stats_fallback.lengthened: 2015 (20 January - 25 March at Fincantieri Palermo — Renaissance Programme Phase 2)
stats_fallback.christened: 22 April 2002 in Genoa (as European Stars for Festival Cruises); no documented separate MSC-era christening with a named godmother
stats_fallback.builder: Chantiers de l'Atlantique, Saint-Nazaire, France (built for Festival Cruises as MS European Stars)
stats_fallback.yacht_club_status: Not installed. The MSC Yacht Club concept debuted on MSC Fantasia in 2008 and has never been installed on the Lirica Class. No Yacht Club retrofit has been announced for Sinfonia or any Lirica-class sister.

related.class_notes: Lirica Class. MSC Sinfonia is the fourth and final Lirica-class ship. Each of the four sisters uses unique Italian-themed venue names: Sinfonia's signature is Il Galeone + Il Covo MDRs and the Teatro San Carlo theatre (named after the historic Naples opera house). Two of the four Lirica-class ships — MSC Sinfonia and MSC Armonia — have a Festival Cruises origin (built 2001-2002 as European Vision and European Stars respectively, acquired by MSC July 2004 after Festival's bankruptcy). The other two (MSC Lirica 2003 and MSC Opera 2004) were ordered directly by MSC. All four received 24-metre lengthening midsections at Fincantieri Palermo between 2014 and 2015 under MSC's Renaissance Programme.

deployment.winter_2025_26: 7-night South America cruises round-trip from Santos (São Paulo), Brazil, through March 2026. Departed Santos 17 March 2026 for transatlantic repositioning to Europe.
deployment.current_ais_2026_04_11: Adriatic Sea, 41.14°N / 16.87°E — approaching Bari, Italy for 11 April 2026 arrival (ETA ~04:00 UTC per VesselFinder).
deployment.summer_2026: Bari, Italy homeport — 7-night Greek Isles round-trips calling at Santorini, Piraeus (Athens), Katakolon, Kefalonia (Argostoli), and Corfu.
deployment.winter_2026_27: Returning to South America (Brazil) as part of MSC's five-ship South America deployment for 2026-27 season.

history.origin: Originally built as MS European Stars for Festival Cruises. Built by Chantiers de l'Atlantique at Saint-Nazaire, France. Delivered 18 April 2002. Christened 22 April 2002 in Genoa as European Stars.
history.msc_acquisition: July 2004 — MSC Cruises acquired European Stars and her sister European Vision (now MSC Armonia) for approximately EUR 220 million following Festival Cruises' bankruptcy.
history.msc_service_start: March 2005 — entered MSC Cruises service as MSC Sinfonia after rebranding drydock.
history.2015_lengthening: 20 January - 25 March 2015 at Fincantieri Palermo, Sicily. Phase 2 of MSC's Renaissance Programme. Ship was cut in half, a 24-metre midsection weighing 2,200-2,400 tonnes was floated in and welded between the two halves. Added 193 passenger cabins and 59 crew cabins. New Doremi Spray Park water-play area, LEGO and Chicco kids' club branding, additional retail and public-space refreshes. Returned to service 25 March 2015 from Palermo via Genoa.
history.2024_01_syracuse_mooring_incident: January 2024 — broke moorings at Syracuse, Italy due to gale-force winds (per CruiseMapper accidents log).
history.2025_04_cancelled_cruise: April 2025 — Mediterranean cruise cancelled due to unspecified technical issues (per CruiseMapper).
history.2023_refit: Late 2023 — class renewal drydock with galley modernization and 'Global Flavors' buffet concept introduced.

discrepancies_flagged.imo_contamination: The previous page had IMO 9250476 — which does not match MSC Sinfonia in any AIS database (5+ sources agree the correct IMO is 9210153). This is the EIGHTH IMO cross-contamination caught across the MSC fleet repair.
discrepancies_flagged.origin_correction: MSC Sinfonia was NOT built fresh in 2005. She was built in 2002 at Chantiers de l'Atlantique as MS European Stars for Festival Cruises, acquired by MSC in July 2004, and entered MSC service in March 2005.
discrepancies_flagged.festival_cruises_pair: Common confusion: Armonia = ex-MS European Vision (2001), Sinfonia = ex-MS European Stars (2002). Both were built at Chantiers de l'Atlantique for Festival Cruises and both were acquired by MSC in the same July 2004 ~EUR 220M deal.
discrepancies_flagged.gt_post_lengthening: Post-2015 lengthening GT is 65,542. MyShipTracking still displays stale pre-lengthening 58,625 GT. The 65,591 GT figure used on MSC Opera's page is slightly different from Sinfonia's 65,542 — each Lirica-class ship has a minor GT variation despite the same lengthening programme.
discrepancies_flagged.beam: VesselFinder, MaritimeOptima, and Wikipedia report 28.8 m (IMO-registered beam); MSC.com and CruiseMapper marketing pages cite 32 m (likely rounded/marketing). Use 28.8 m as authoritative.
discrepancies_flagged.cabins_added_2015: Verified sources consistently say 193 passenger cabins added in 2015 (not 194).
discrepancies_flagged.crew_count: Verified sources consistently say ~721 (not 728 as some references suggest).
discrepancies_flagged.godmother: Sophia Loren is NOT listed among MSC Sinfonia's christening in the available sources — she christened Lirica, Opera, Musica, Preziosa, Seaview, and Seashore, but no reliable source confirms her as Sinfonia's MSC-era godmother. The original 2002 Festival Cruises christening of European Stars took place in Genoa on 22 April 2002; the Festival-era godmother identity is not captured in available sources. DO NOT attribute Sophia Loren without stronger sourcing.
discrepancies_flagged.hallucinations_removed: Previous Review JSON-LD mentioned 'La Caravella and Il Covo restaurants' — La Caravella is MSC Opera's MDR, not Sinfonia's. Sinfonia's MDRs are Il Galeone + Il Covo. Also mentioned 'panoramic Top Sail lounge' — Top Sail Lounge is an MSC Yacht Club venue and the Lirica class does not have Yacht Club.
